meta.subject: Plaque psoriasis
meta.scope: Plaque psoriasis (psoriasis vulgaris). Commercial core = moderate-to-severe, systemic-eligible adults and adolescents aged 12+; all lines of systemic therapy, plus the branded topical segment where it competes for the same patient.
meta.region_focus: Global, with US and EU5 depth

snapshot.value: Plaque psoriasis (psoriasis vulgaris). Commercial core: moderate-to-severe, systemic-eligible adults and adolescents 12+. Branded topicals included where they compete for the same patient.
Immunology / I&I — chronic, relapsing, lifelong; assets are cross-indication (PsA, HS, IBD, axSpA)
Global, with US and EU5 depth
43.0M cases (GBD 2021), up 86% from 23.1M in 1990; projected ~60.3M by 2036. Widely quoted WHO-era figure of "60M+ today" is not supported by the GBD 2021 point estimate.
~3.0% of adults ≈ 7.6M with psoriasis; ~6.5M with the plaque phenotype [Estimated]. Claims-based studies give 0.5–0.7% — a 4–6× divergence that is a definitional artefact, not a real disagreement.
~1.6M systemic-eligible → ~1.1M diagnosed and in specialist care → ~510k on advanced therapy [Estimated; see Section 3]

Topicals → phototherapy or conventional systemics (methotrexate, ciclosporin, acitretin) → advanced therapy. Advanced therapy is now led by IL-23 inhibitors (risankizumab, guselkumab, tildrakizumab), IL-17 inhibitors (secukinumab, ixekizumab, bimekizumab, brodalumab), with TNF inhibitors largely displaced to biosimilar economics, and an oral tier (apremilast, deucravacitinib, and from March 2026 icotrokinra).
Roughly half of diagnosed, systemic-eligible patients never reach advanced therapy. This is an access, route and step-edit failure, not an efficacy failure — the field already clears skin.
~$30B global (2025); ~$32B (2026) [Estimated: bottom-up brand build reconciled against published top-down envelopes of $28.9–34.2B]
7.8% (2026→2031, base case); 5.3% (2026→2036) as injectable IL-23 loses exclusivity in the early 2030s

IL-23: Skyrizi ($17.56B FY25, all indications), Tremfya ($5.2B), Stelara (~$6.0B, collapsing), Ilumya. IL-17: Cosentyx ($6.7B), Taltz, Bimzelx (€2.22B), Siliq. TNF: Humira ($4.54B, −26%), Enbrel, plus biosimilars. Oral: Otezla, Sotyktu (~$0.3B), Icotyde (launched 2026). Topical: Zoryve, Vtama ($128M).

3 next-generation oral systemics at or through filing — icotrokinra (approved Mar 2026), zasocitinib (NDA from FY2026), envudeucitinib (NDA 2H 2026) — plus ≥5 China-origin Phase 3 assets and a Lilly oral IL-17 in Phase 2
AbbVie, Johnson & Johnson, Novartis, UCB, Eli Lilly, Bristol Myers Squibb, Takeda, Alumis, Sun Pharma, Amgen

segments.label: Injectable IL-23 (risankizumab, guselkumab, tildrakizumab, ustekinumab originator)
segments.note: Volume growth continues to 2031; risankizumab and guselkumab LOE in the early 2030s drives the ten-year decline. [Estimated]

segments.label: IL-17 (secukinumab, ixekizumab, bimekizumab, brodalumab)
segments.note: Bimekizumab growth roughly offsets secukinumab and ixekizumab maturity; secukinumab LOE early 2030s. [Estimated]

segments.label: Oral targeted systemic (apremilast, deucravacitinib, icotrokinra, zasocitinib, envudeucitinib)
segments.note: Assumes icotrokinra reaches ~$4B and the TYK2 class ~$5B by 2031, and that orals expand the treated pool rather than only switching it. Apremilast generic from Feb 2028. [Estimated]

segments.label: TNF inhibitors and biosimilars (incl. ustekinumab biosimilars)
segments.note: Private-label biosimilar economics compress the whole tier; PBM formulary displacement already underway. [Estimated]

segments.label: Topical and conventional (roflumilast, tapinarof, generic topicals, phototherapy, conventional systemics)
segments.note: Branded non-steroidal topicals take share from generic steroids; paediatric label expansion. [Estimated]

note: [Estimated] throughout. Base year 2026 = $32.0B, derived as described above. CAGR computed as (end/start)^(1/years) − 1 over the stated horizon.

scenarios.name: Base
scenarios.swing: (1) Advanced-therapy penetration in diagnosed systemic-eligible patients rises from ~45% to ~58%; (2) blended net price erodes ~3%/yr; (3) icotrokinra secures a genuine first-line payer position in year 2; (4) no major LOE inside the window.

scenarios.name: Conservative
scenarios.swing: (1) Orals cannibalise injectables rather than expanding the pool — penetration stalls near 48%; (2) payers position the oral tier behind conventional-systemic AND biologic failure, repeating the Sotyktu experience; (3) net price erodes 6%/yr as three orals compete for the same formulary slot; (4) IRA selection extends to an IL-23 franchise.

scenarios.name: Aggressive
scenarios.swing: (1) Penetration reaches ~70% as oral-first sequencing is adopted in guidelines; (2) adolescent and high-impact-site labels open new segments; (3) net price holds because efficacy tiering is defended on PASI 100 data; (4) a comorbidity-outcomes readout justifies earlier intervention.

funnel.basis: 3.0% adult prevalence (NHANES 2011–14, Armstrong 2021) applied to ~255M US adults
25% of plaque patients (BSA >10%, PASI ≥10, or high-impact site)

funnel.note: [Estimated]. Attrition is dermatologist access and referral, not disease severity.

funnel.stage: On advanced therapy (biologic or targeted oral)
funnel.note: [Estimated]. Reconciles to ~$19B US market at ~$38k annual net cost per patient.

funnel.stage: Systemic-eligible, diagnosed, NOT on advanced therapy
funnel.note: The core opportunity. 31.7% of moderate-to-severe patients report using only OTC, complementary or topical therapy.

segmentation.why: The contested first-line segment. Highest lifetime value; the segment icotrokinra's label targets directly.
Annual switch rate of 15–20% keeps this liquid. Won on PASI 100 and durability, not on convenience.
Drives choice toward assets with a PsA label — IL-17s, IL-23s, TNFs, and now deucravacitinib (approved for PsA in 2026). Removes the pure-derm orals from consideration unless they add PsA.

segmentation.segment: High-impact site disease (scalp, genital, palmoplantar, nail)
segmentation.why: Severe functional burden at low BSA; systematically excluded by BSA-threshold payer criteria. No dedicated approvals — a live label opportunity.
Small revenue, disproportionate strategic value: first-line habit formation in a lifelong disease. Icotrokinra approved from 12.
Weight-based dosing failure with older agents; worse response rates; the population where a cardiometabolic outcomes claim would be most valuable.

opportunity.gap: Diagnosed, systemic-eligible, not on advanced therapy
opportunity.the_gap: Access, route aversion, step therapy — not efficacy
opportunity.size: ~$3.7B US / $7–8B global over 5y [Estimated: 170k US converts × $22k net]

opportunity.gap: Incomplete clearance on current therapy (not at PASI 100)
opportunity.the_gap: Depth of response; drives 15–20% annual switching
opportunity.size: Switching value, not new market. ~$1.5B of contested revenue annually [Estimated]

opportunity.the_gap: No dedicated indication or site-specific endpoint precedent
opportunity.size: Label differentiation rather than a separate market; supports premium positioning
Reframes the category; would reduce near-term revenue while capturing share. Strategic, not additive

opportunity.the_gap: No prognostic biomarker; no prevention indication
opportunity.population: ~30% of psoriasis patients over a lifetime
opportunity.size: Would create a new, biomarker-defined segment. Unquantifiable until a biomarker validates

opportunity.the_gap: No outcomes trial or label claim
opportunity.size: Highest leverage on payer behaviour of any evidence in the indication

opportunity.the_gap: Most agents defer paediatric data post-approval
opportunity.size: Small direct revenue; disproportionate lifetime value through first-line habit formation
~$2B combined today; volume-led growth at a sixth of US net price

payers.coverage: Broad for IL-23 and IL-17 after step therapy; new agents face tighter PA in year 1
payers.hta: PBM formulary committees (CVS Caremark, Optum Rx, Express Scripts)
payers.restriction: Step therapy through phototherapy or a conventional systemic; incomplete step documentation is the leading PA denial reason
payers.net_price: Blended net ~$38k/yr [Est] and eroding; reference resetting to ~$20k as biosimilars and MFPs land

payers.coverage: Covered; MFPs now in force for selected agents
payers.restriction: Stelara MFP $4,695 vs $13,836 list from Jan 2026; Otezla MFP from Jan 2027
payers.net_price: Public reference price −66% to −67% off list

payers.restriction: PASI ≥10 AND DLQI >10 required; adequate response defined as 75% PASI reduction; formally excludes most moderate disease
payers.net_price: Confidential patient-access schemes; effective net well below list

payers.coverage: Broad access at launch, then price-negotiated
payers.restriction: Additional-benefit assessment determines reimbursed price; class-lumping is common where no head-to-head exists
payers.net_price: AMNOG-negotiated; head-to-head data is the main defence against class reference pricing

payers.restriction: ASMR rating drives price; sequencing requirements apply
Biennial price revisions erode net systematically
Steep negotiated discounts required for listing
payers.net_price: ~$6k/yr [Est] — roughly a sixth of US net

attribution.access: Broad first-line coverage; the reference agent against which orals are stepped
Mature; growth now from HS and the US IV formulation, not derm
IRA MFP $4,695 vs $13,836 list from Jan 2026; 7 US / 8 EU biosimilars at up to 85% discount
Removed from Big-3 PBM 2026 formularies in favour of private-label biosimilars

attribution.fy_rev: Not separately disclosed in FY2025 release; guided flat-to-declining for 2026
attribution.access: Price realisation declining per Lilly Q1 2026 commentary

attribution.fy_rev: Not retrieved for FY2025; declining from ~$2.2B (2024)
attribution.access: Selected for IRA IPAY 2027 — MFP effective 1 Jan 2027
Moved to BMS Patient Connect DTC at >80% below list from Jan 2026
First-line systemic label; new-agent PA restrictions expected through year 1
Price-positioned challenger in the IL-23 class
#1 prescribed branded topical across AD, seb derm and psoriasis combined; cream to age 2, foam to age 12
Acquired with Dermavant Q4 2024; Canada psoriasis launch Q3 2025

loe.consequence: Humira down 26.1% in FY2025 to $4.54B. Big-3 PBMs removed the reference product for 2026, favouring private-label biosimilars.

loe.event: US biosimilar entry (7 approved US / 8 EU)
loe.consequence: Discounts up to 85%; Stelara down ~42% in 2025 and ~48% in Q4 alone. Sets the net-price floor for the whole IL-23 tier.
$4,695 vs $13,836 list (−66%). A public reference price for an IL-23 agent — the most consequential single number in the access landscape.
$2,355 vs $7,106 (−67%). Largely rheumatology-relevant but reinforces the reference-pricing precedent.
Sets the Medicare reference for the oral tier ahead of icotrokinra and TYK2 scale-up.
Amgen's patent win blocks Sandoz and Zydus until Feb 2028; one asserted patent runs to 2034. Post-2028 a generic oral becomes the cheapest mandatory step before any new oral.
The dominant driver of the ten-year forecast; the reason base-case CAGR decays from 7.8% (5y) to 5.3% (10y).

competitive.depth: Thin in psoriasis — no oral, no announced next-gen derm asset
competitive.posture: Defend the IL-23 franchise; grow via IBD
competitive.strength: Scale, prescriber entrenchment, rebate leverage; absorbed $16B of Humira erosion and still grew
competitive.vulnerability: No oral answer to icotrokinra; LOE early 2030s; single-asset concentration

competitive.position: Co-leader and best-placed for the next cycle. Tremfya $5.2B (+40.5%); Icotyde launched Mar 2026
competitive.depth: Deepest — owns both injectable and oral IL-23
competitive.posture: Replace the biosimilarised Stelara franchise with an oral-plus-injectable IL-23 pair
competitive.strength: Only approved oral with biologic-class efficacy; first-line label; adolescent coverage; head-to-head superiority data
competitive.vulnerability: Stelara down ~42% in 2025 (−1,040bps on Immunology); launch execution risk against rebate-protected incumbents

competitive.depth: Limited in psoriasis; growth from HS and IV formulation
competitive.posture: Harvest and extend via new indications and formulations
competitive.strength: Broad label set; established prescriber base; IV option
competitive.vulnerability: Derm growth has stalled; exposed to oral substitution; LOE early 2030s

competitive.position: Challenger with the best injectable efficacy. Bimzelx €2.22B (>200% growth)
competitive.depth: Moderate — indication expansion rather than new molecules
competitive.posture: Efficacy-led share capture across five indications
competitive.strength: Highest PASI 100 among injectables; >116k patients; ≥€4B peak target
competitive.vulnerability: IL-17 is the class most directly substitutable by an efficacious oral; candidiasis and IBD-caution signals

competitive.position: Mature IL-17 (Taltz) plus the only oral IL-17 programme
competitive.depth: One Phase 2 oral IL-17 (LY4100511) after discontinuing DC-806
competitive.posture: Manage Taltz decline; re-enter via oral IL-17
competitive.strength: Only credible oral IL-17 mechanism; deep immunology capability
competitive.vulnerability: Taltz guided flat-to-declining; wrote off the first DICE asset; a generation behind the oral leaders

competitive.posture: Salvage volume via a direct-to-patient discount channel
competitive.strength: First-in-class TYK2 with 5-year durability and a new PsA label
competitive.vulnerability: Proof that efficacy without payer position yields no revenue; DTC pricing destroys the class's price reference

competitive.depth: One late-stage oral plus PsA Phase 3 and IBD Phase 2
competitive.posture: Buy into I&I at scale via the $4B Nimbus deal
competitive.strength: Only PASI 100 head-to-head win over another oral; fastest onset in class; cross-indication optionality
competitive.vulnerability: Second into the segment; class-lumped with cheaper deucravacitinib by payers

competitive.strength: Competitive PASI 90/100 data; Kaken deal validates the asset
competitive.vulnerability: No commercial infrastructure; no head-to-head against another oral; financing dependence
IRA MFP 2027, generics Feb 2028; becomes a payer obstacle rather than an asset

competitive.company: China cohort (Innovent, Junshi, Hansoh, InnoCare, BIOCAD)
competitive.position: Domestic Phase 3 across IL-23, IL-17 and TYK2
competitive.posture: Serve a <10% penetrated domestic market; out-licence opportunistically
competitive.strength: Cost base; adolescent programmes; completed pivotals
competitive.vulnerability: No Western regulatory path yet; mechanisms already commoditised in the US and EU

catalysts_watch.catalyst: Icotyde (icotrokinra) US launch trajectory — first two quarters of scripts and payer coverage
catalysts_watch.why: The single cleanest test of whether an oral with biologic-class efficacy can win a first-line position against rebate-protected injectables. If it repeats Sotyktu's launch curve, the oral thesis is worth materially less.

catalysts_watch.catalyst: Zasocitinib NDA submission and FDA acceptance
catalysts_watch.why: Second next-generation oral into the queue; LATITUDE Atlas showed statistical superiority over deucravacitinib on PASI 100 at week 16, making it the first oral to beat another oral head-to-head.
Third oral entrant; PASI 100 of ~40% at week 24 is competitive. Alumis is the only pure-play, making it the most likely acquisition target in the class.
First head-to-head of an oral against an injectable biologic in plaque psoriasis. A superiority win reframes guideline sequencing.

catalysts_watch.catalyst: Otezla maximum fair price under IRA IPAY 2027
catalysts_watch.why: Sets the Medicare reference price for the oral tier and anchors commercial negotiation for icotrokinra and the TYK2s.
Removes the branded floor from the oral segment and creates a cheap step-therapy rung payers can require before any new oral.

catalysts_watch.catalyst: 2027 PBM formulary decisions on Icotyde and the TYK2 class
catalysts_watch.why: Determines whether the oral tier is positioned first-line or behind biologic failure. This decision, not the clinical data, sets the segment's 2027–2031 revenue.
The single largest determinant of the ten-year forecast; the reason base-case CAGR decays from 7.8% (5y) to 5.3% (10y).

thesis.heading: State of the field
thesis.text: Plaque psoriasis is the most commercially mature autoimmune dermatology indication and, as of 2026, the one undergoing the sharpest structural change in a decade. Two things happened in parallel. First, the injectable ceiling was reached: IL-23 and IL-17 inhibitors deliver PASI 90 in 55–75% of patients and complete clearance in 40–60%, and the marginal gain from the next antibody is small enough that head-to-head superiority trials now turn on PASI 100 rather than PASI 75. Second, on 18 March 2026 the FDA approved icotrokinra (ICOTYDE, Johnson & Johnson) — the first targeted oral peptide antagonist of the IL-23 receptor — for moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis in adults and patients 12 and over weighing at least 40 kg. For the first time an oral agent carries biologic-class efficacy rather than the compromised efficacy of apremilast or, in commercial practice, deucravacitinib. The consequence is that the competitive question has moved from "which molecule clears skin best" to "which molecule gets to the patient first, at what net price". That is a payer question, and the payer environment simultaneously hardened: ustekinumab biosimilars entered the US in February 2025 at discounts up to 85%; the Big Three PBMs removed Humira and Stelara reference products from 2026 formularies in favour of their own private-label biosimilars; and the Inflation Reduction Act set Stelara's maximum fair price at $4,695 versus a $13,836 list from 1 January 2026, with Otezla following in 2027.

thesis.heading: Market size and growth trajectory
thesis.text: We size the global plaque psoriasis therapeutics market at approximately $30B in 2025 and $32B in 2026 [Estimated], built bottom-up from indication-attributable brand revenue and cross-checked against a top-down envelope of $28.9–34.2B from published market-research estimates. Base case takes the market to ~$40B by 2029 and ~$46.5B by 2031 — a 7.8% CAGR over five years — before growth decelerates to a 5.3% ten-year CAGR as risankizumab and secukinumab approach loss of exclusivity in the early 2030s. Growth is almost entirely a mix shift, not a price story: the oral targeted segment grows from ~$3B to ~$14B by 2031 (36% CAGR) while the TNF and biosimilar tier contracts at roughly −15% a year. The headline growth figures circulating in market-research reports — up to $80B by 2034 — are not reconcilable with observed brand revenue and appear to be built on list rather than net prices. Treat them as an upper bound on gross sales, not as a forecast of realisable revenue.

thesis.heading: Competitive landscape highlights
thesis.text: AbbVie is the clear leader: Skyrizi reached $17.56B in FY2025 (+32.5% in Q4 alone) inside a $30.4B immunology portfolio, and did so in only the second full year after the Humira LOE. Johnson & Johnson holds the strongest strategic position for the next cycle — Tremfya grew 40.5% to $5.2B and icotrokinra gives it the first oral IL-23 franchise — but it is simultaneously absorbing a Stelara collapse of roughly 42% in 2025, a ~1,040 basis-point drag on Immunology segment growth. Novartis (Cosentyx, $6.7B) and Lilly (Taltz) are defending mature IL-17 franchises with lifecycle indications rather than new derm efficacy. UCB is the momentum story: Bimzelx reached €2.22B in FY2025, over 200% growth, with 53% of sales from psoriasis. Bristol Myers Squibb is the cautionary tale and the single most instructive data point in this report. Sotyktu, an oral with genuine efficacy and a clean safety profile, reached only ~$0.3B in FY2025 with US revenue flat as higher demand was offset by lower net selling price. BMS's response — routing Sotyktu through a direct-to-patient platform at more than 80% below list from January 2026 — is an admission that the rebate-gated channel would not give an oral a first-line position. Every oral entrant now faces that same wall.

thesis.heading: Key strategic risks and access barriers
thesis.text: The dominant risk is not clinical. It is that the oral era expands the number of competitors without expanding the treated pool — in which case the new entrants cannibalise injectables at a lower net price and the market grows more slowly than the patient count. Step therapy through conventional systemics remains near-universal in the US, and incomplete step documentation is the leading cause of prior-authorisation denial. In Europe, NICE-derived thresholds (PASI ≥10 and DLQI >10) formally exclude most of the moderate segment from biologic access. Meanwhile PBM economics have inverted: private-label biosimilars from Cordavis, Nuvaila and Quallent are displacing both originators and third-party biosimilars, which compresses net price across the whole ustekinumab and adalimumab tier and resets the reference point against which any new oral is priced.

thesis.heading: Investment thesis
thesis.text: Plaque psoriasis remains an attractive market on absolute size and durability but has become a poor market for undifferentiated entry. The value now sits in two places: (1) assets that credibly convert untreated systemic-eligible patients rather than switch already-treated ones — which means oral route, first-line label, adolescent coverage and site-specific data; and (2) capabilities that lower the cost of proving differentiation in an indication where the efficacy bar is already ~70% IGA 0/1 and trials must therefore be large, head-to-head and endpoint-aggressive. A ninth me-too IL-17 or IL-23 antibody has negative expected value. A tenth oral entrant without a payer strategy has the same problem Sotyktu had.

deals_report.read: Development financing rather than a commercialisation partnership. Signals Alumis is positioning to be acquired, not to launch.
Produced $128M FY2025. Branded topicals support acquisition economics at modest scale.
Lead asset DC-806 discontinued within a year; programme continues with LY4100511 in Phase 2. A cautionary precedent on paying Phase-1 prices for oral mechanism risk.
Vindicated by Phase 3 success in Dec 2025 and the PASI 100 head-to-head win over deucravacitinib. The benchmark for what an oral in this class is worth pre-Phase 3.
Now facing an IRA maximum fair price in 2027 and generic entry in Feb 2028. Instructive on the durability of oral franchises in this indication.
Payers deploying capital into supply. The most consequential structural change in the indication's economics and the least discussed.

risks.risk: Orals cannibalise injectables instead of expanding the treated pool, so the market grows with price rather than volume
risks.trigger: Icotyde's first four quarters showing net new patient starts rather than switches from biologics; NBRx mix disclosed by J&J

risks.risk: Payers step the oral tier behind biologic failure, repeating the Sotyktu outcome
risks.trigger: Published 2027 formulary criteria placing icotrokinra at parity with or ahead of an IL-23 biologic on at least one Big-3 formulary

risks.risk: Net-price compression accelerates as three orals compete for one formulary slot
risks.trigger: Whether zasocitinib and envudeucitinib price to differentiate or to buy access; whether DTC channels proliferate beyond BMS

risks.risk: IRA selection extends to an IL-23 franchise (risankizumab or guselkumab)
risks.trigger: CMS selection lists in subsequent IPAY cycles; Part D spend rankings

risks.risk: PBM private-label biosimilar vertical integration further compresses the whole class reference price
risks.trigger: Regulatory or legislative action on PBM ownership of supply; any rebate-reform proposal advancing

risks.risk: Class safety signal in oral TYK2 inhibitors (a JAK-adjacent finding at scale)
risks.trigger: Post-marketing surveillance across deucravacitinib, zasocitinib and envudeucitinib; any FDA class labelling action

risks.risk: Icotrokinra real-world efficacy underperforms the ICONIC trial population
risks.trigger: First registry and claims-based persistence data; 12-month drug-survival curves versus IL-23 biologics

risks.risk: Biosimilar entry to risankizumab and secukinumab arrives earlier than the early-2030s assumption
risks.trigger: Patent litigation outcomes and biosimilar filing disclosures; any settlement dates becoming public

risks.risk: China-origin assets enter Western markets at disruptive price points via in-licensing
risks.trigger: Any Western regulatory filing by Innovent, Junshi, Hansoh or InnoCare in this indication

risks.risk: Epidemiology overstated — the addressable pool is closer to claims-based than survey-based prevalence
risks.trigger: Prospective claims-linked prevalence work; whether icotrokinra's conversion of untreated patients matches the funnel's implied ~630k
